content:
{
  "title": "Counter-Example-Guided Prompting for Robust Mathematical Reasoning in LLMs",
  "problem": "Current LLM prompting techniques for mathematical problem solving often encourage single-pass, forward-only reasoning, making models brittle to subtle mistakes or reasoning oversights. This limits their ability to self-correct and robustly solve multi-step or tricky mathematical problems.",
  "motivation": "Improving mathematical reasoning in LLMs is crucial for reliable AI deployment in education, scientific research, and technical domains. Prior work has mostly focused on chain-of-thought or self-consistency, but little attention has been given to reflection upon and correction of erroneous intermediate steps. Enabling models to interrogate and refine their own outputs based on principled error discovery is a timely and potentially transformative approach.",
  "method": "We propose a novel prompting method where, after the model generates an initial solution, it is prompted to construct plausible counter-examples or adversarial cases that could invalidate key steps in its own reasoning. The model is then required to analyze whether its original chain-of-thought withstands these counter-examples, and if not, iteratively revise its solution until no counter-examples can be found. This creates a closed-loop, adversarial self-consistency dynamic within the LLM’s own reasoning process, encouraging deeper scrutiny and correction of subtle errors.",
  "experiment_plan": "We will evaluate on GSM8K and MATH datasets, comparing our method to standard chain-of-thought, self-consistency, and scratchpad prompting. Metrics will include final answer accuracy, error detection rate, and solution edit distance. Ablation studies will assess the contributions of counter-example generation and iterative refinement."
}
